content.meaning: e.g. He sometimes goes swimming.
e.g. She works in a bank.
(timetables, programmes, etc.) e.g. The match begins at 8.30 next Monday evening.
e.g. Ice melts when heated.
e.g. First you chop the meat, then you fry it.
e.g. He's sleeping at the moment. Prices are rising nowadays.

content.word: Present Cont.: repeated action with always
content.meaning: (often to show annoyance) e.g. He's always biting his nails.

content.word: Present Cont.: definite arrangement or plan
content.meaning: e.g. They are going on an excursion tomorrow.
e.g. She has just painted her room.

content.word: Present Perfect: action beginning in the past and continuing up to the present
content.meaning: e.g. I haven't seen him for years.
e.g. She has been to Rome. (We do not know when)

content.word: Present Perfect: repeated action still continuing
content.meaning: e.g. He has worked as a teacher for four years. (He is still a teacher.)

content.word: Present Perfect Cont.: past action producing visible result in the present
content.meaning: e.g. She has been painting her room. (It smells of paint.)

content.word: Present Perfect Cont.: duration of an action beginning in the past and continuing up to the present
content.meaning: e.g. She has been waiting for two hours but there's still no sign of him.

content.word: Present Perfect Cont.: action showing annoyance, explanation, irritation or surprise
content.meaning: e.g. What have you been searching for in my desk?
Stative verbs describe a permanent state and do not have continuous forms. These include verbs of: senses, thinking, emotion & feelings, other.
see, hear, smell, feel, taste
agree, believe, consider, doubt, expect, feel (=think)
feel, forgive, hate, loathe, like, love, mind, wish, etc.
appear/seem, be, belong, have (=possess), keep (=continue), matter, owe, possess / own
Verbs of the senses can have continuous forms, but then there's a change in meaning. I'm seeing my dentist tomorrow. (=I'm visiting) but: Do you see those birds? (ability to see)
The verbs think, consider and expect can have continuous forms when they refer to an activity. Be quiet, please! I'm thinking. but: I think you are wrong. (= I believe)

Have can have continuous forms in certain expressions such as: have a bath, have a nap, have fun, etc. We were having a good time at the party before the fire broke out. but: Since you have his phone number, you can call him. (= possess)
